az.plot_posterior(coin_trace, var_names=["p"])
plt.show()

Exercise 2. Modify the height model from this chapter to use a Student-t likelihood instead of Normal. Use pm.StudentT with nu=4 degrees of freedom. Compare the posterior estimates of mu and sigma to the Normal-likelihood version. Which model has wider credible intervals? Why?






Solution






with pm.Model() as robust_model:
    mu = pm.Normal("mu", mu=170, sigma=20)
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal("sigma", sigma=10)

    # Student-t likelihood (heavier tails)
    y_obs = pm.StudentT(
        "y_obs",
        nu=4,
        mu=mu,
        sigma=sigma,
        observed=heights,
    )

    robust_trace = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
    )

print("--- Student-t model ---")
print(az.summary(robust_trace, var_names=["mu", "sigma"]))

The Student-t model typically produces slightly wider credible intervals because the heavier-tailed likelihood allows for more variation in the data. Each observation carries less “certainty” than it would under a Normal likelihood.



Exercise 3. Run a prior predictive check on the coin model from Exercise 1. Plot a histogram of simulated “number of heads” from the prior. Does the prior generate plausible data?






Solution






with coin_model:
    coin_prior = pm.sample_prior_predictive(
        samples=1000, random_seed=42
    )

prior_heads = (
    coin_prior.prior_predictive["y"].values.flatten()
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.hist(
    prior_heads,
    bins=range(0, 52),
    alpha=0.7,
    color="steelblue",
    edgecolor="white",
)
ax.axvline(
    x=n_heads, color="red", linestyle="--",
    label=f"Observed ({n_heads} heads)",
)
ax.set_xlabel("Number of heads (out of 50)")
ax.set_ylabel("Frequency")
ax.set_title("Prior Predictive: Coin Model")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








With a Beta(2, 2) prior, the model generates head counts spread across the full range 0–50, with a mild concentration near 25. This is reasonable: before seeing data, we are fairly uncertain but lean toward a fair coin. The observed value of 32 sits comfortably within the prior predictive range, confirming the prior is not excluding plausible outcomes.



Exercise 4. You measure the waiting time (in minutes) between buses at a stop. You record 20 observations: [4.2, 7.1, 3.8, 5.5, 6.3, 4.9, 8.2, 3.1, 5.8, 7.4,   4.6, 6.1, 5.3, 9.0, 4.4, 6.7, 3.5, 5.9, 7.8, 4.1]. Build a model assuming wait times follow an Exponential distribution. Use a pm.Gamma prior on the rate parameter. Sample the posterior, plot the trace, and run a posterior predictive check.






Solution






wait_times = np.array([
    4.2, 7.1, 3.8, 5.5, 6.3, 4.9, 8.2, 3.1,
    5.8, 7.4, 4.6, 6.1, 5.3, 9.0, 4.4, 6.7,
    3.5, 5.9, 7.8, 4.1,
])

with pm.Model() as bus_model:
    # Gamma(2, 1) prior on rate: mean=2, moderate uncertainty
    # rate = 1/mean_wait_time
    rate = pm.Gamma("rate", alpha=2, beta=10)

    # Exponential likelihood
    obs = pm.Exponential(
        "obs", lam=rate, observed=wait_times
    )

    bus_trace = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
    )

    bus_ppc = pm.sample_posterior_predictive(
        bus_trace, random_seed=42
    )

# Diagnostics
print(az.summary(bus_trace, var_names=["rate"]))
az.plot_trace(bus_trace, var_names=["rate"])
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Posterior predictive check
az.plot_ppc(bus_ppc, figsize=(8, 4))
plt.title("PPC: Bus Wait Times")
plt.show()

rate    1.0  
















You may notice the posterior predictive check shows some mismatch—the Exponential distribution always peaks at zero, but the observed data peaks around 4–6 minutes. This suggests a Gamma or LogNormal likelihood might be more appropriate. Posterior predictive checks revealing model inadequacy is exactly how they are supposed to work.









Prediction without refitting: the mutable data pattern



A practical pattern Fonnesbeck highlights is mutable data. After fitting a model, you often want predictions on new inputs without re-running the sampler. PyMC’s pm.Data containers let you swap observed data after fitting:
with pm.Model() as model:
    x = pm.Data("x", x_train)
    # ... define priors and likelihood using x ...
    trace = pm.sample()

    # Swap in new data, no re-sampling needed
    pm.set_data({"x": x_new})
    ppc = pm.sample_posterior_predictive(trace)
This is critical for practical workflows — you fit once, then generate posterior predictive samples for as many new inputs as you like.








Turning the Bayesian crank



Fonnesbeck captures a key Bayesian insight: “You can always turn the Bayesian crank — take your posterior as the prior for new data.” This is sequential updating in action. When new data arrives, you do not start over; your posterior from the first batch of data becomes your prior for the second batch. The math guarantees that updating sequentially gives the same result as updating all at once (we proved this in Chapter 1, Exercise 1). In practice, this means Bayesian models learn continuously.
crumbs: 2  PyMC Fundamentals: Model Building and Sampling

title: 2  PyMC Fundamentals: Model Building and Sampling
text:
2.12 Chapter Summary
You now know the core PyMC workflow:

Define a model inside with pm.Model().
Specify priors for unknown parameters using PyMC distributions.
Connect data using observed= in the likelihood.
Check priors with pm.sample_prior_predictive().
Sample the posterior with pm.sample().
Diagnose with az.summary(), az.plot_trace(), and R-hat/ESS.
Validate with pm.sample_posterior_predictive() and az.plot_ppc().
Track derived quantities with pm.Deterministic().

In the next chapter, we put this workflow to use on a real modelling problem: linear regression, the Bayesian way.
crumbs: 2  PyMC Fundamentals: Model Building and Sampling

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.1 OLS Refresher
You already know linear regression. Or you did, once. You learned \(y = X\beta + \varepsilon\) in a statistics class, ran it in Excel or Python, got a table of coefficients and \(p\)-values, and moved on. That knowledge is the foundation for everything in this chapter.
What we are going to do here is take that familiar model and rebuild it as a Bayesian model in PyMC. You will see that the answers barely change — the posterior means will land right on top of the OLS estimates. But the questions you can ask become richer. Instead of a single best-fit line, you get a distribution over regression lines. Instead of confidence intervals derived from asymptotic theory, you get the full posterior uncertainty over every parameter. And instead of a framework that struggles with outliers, missing data, and small samples, you get one that handles them naturally.
The payoff is not dramatic for simple problems. That is the point. Start where the answers are easy to verify, so you can trust the machinery when the problems get hard.
Linear regression models the relationship between a response variable \(y\) and one or more predictors \(X\) as:
\[
y = X\beta + \varepsilon, \qquad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)
\]
The Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) estimator finds the \(\hat{\beta}\) that minimizes the sum of squared residuals. In matrix form, the closed-form solution is \(\hat{\beta} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y\). No iteration, no tuning — plug in the data and out comes an answer. That simplicity is what makes OLS the workhorse of applied statistics.
But notice the assumptions baked into that formula. The errors \(\varepsilon\) are normally distributed, have constant variance (homoscedasticity), and are independent of each other. When these assumptions hold, OLS is hard to beat. When they break down — outliers, heteroscedasticity, correlated errors, small samples — the guarantees evaporate. Keep that in mind as we move through the chapter.
Statsmodels gives you OLS in a few lines. Let us generate some data we can use throughout the chapter. We will simulate a simple relationship: house price (in thousands of dollars) as a function of square footage, with Gaussian noise.
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.api as sm

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# Simulate data: price = 50 + 0.15 * sqft + noise
n = 80
sqft = rng.uniform(800, 3000, size=n)
true_intercept = 50.0
true_slope = 0.15
noise_sd = 30.0
price = true_intercept + true_slope * sqft + rng.normal(0, noise_sd, size=n)

df = pd.DataFrame({"sqft": sqft, "price": price})
df.head()

price

Now fit OLS with statsmodels:
X_ols = sm.add_constant(df["sqft"])
ols_model = sm.OLS(df["price"], X_ols).fit()
print(ols_model.summary())

strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.
The summary gives you point estimates for the intercept and slope, their standard errors, \(t\)-statistics, \(p\)-values, and 95% confidence intervals. For this clean, well-behaved data, OLS works perfectly. The estimates should land close to the true values we used to generate the data (\(\beta_0 = 50\), \(\beta_1 = 0.15\), \(\sigma = 30\)).
Let us visualize the fit:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(df["sqft"], df["price"], alpha=0.5, s=30, color="steelblue")

x_grid = np.linspace(700, 3100, 200)
X_pred = sm.add_constant(x_grid)
pred = ols_model.get_prediction(X_pred)
pred_summary = pred.summary_frame(alpha=0.05)

ax.plot(x_grid, pred_summary["mean"], color="black", linewidth=2)
ax.fill_between(
    x_grid,
    pred_summary["obs_ci_lower"],
    pred_summary["obs_ci_upper"],
    alpha=0.15,
    color="steelblue",
    label="95% prediction interval",
)
ax.fill_between(
    x_grid,
    pred_summary["mean_ci_lower"],
    pred_summary["mean_ci_upper"],
    alpha=0.3,
    color="steelblue",
    label="95% confidence interval",
)
ax.set_xlabel("Square footage")
ax.set_ylabel("Price ($k)")
ax.set_title("OLS fit: Price vs. Square Footage")
ax.legend(loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 3.1: OLS regression line with 95% confidence band.
This is the baseline. Everything that follows should reproduce these results — and then go beyond them.
Take a moment to notice what OLS gives you: a single best-fit line, a table of standard errors derived from the residual variance, and confidence intervals computed from the \(t\)-distribution. These are all functions of the data and the model assumptions. If the assumptions hold, they are exactly right. If they do not, you have no easy way to know how far off they are. The Bayesian approach will give us a different way to think about these same quantities.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.2 The Bayesian Linear Model
The Bayesian version of linear regression uses the same likelihood:
\[
y_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i,\; \sigma^2)
\]
The difference is that we place prior distributions on the unknown parameters \(\beta_0\), \(\beta_1\), and \(\sigma\). Instead of computing a single point estimate, Bayes’ theorem gives us a posterior distribution over all three parameters simultaneously:
\[
p(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma \mid y, X) \propto
  p(y \mid X, \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma) \;
  p(\beta_0) \; p(\beta_1) \; p(\sigma)
\]
Think of it this way. OLS draws one line through the data — the line that minimizes squared error. The Bayesian model draws many lines, each weighted by how well it explains the data and how plausible it is under the priors. The posterior is the full collection of plausible regression lines, not just the single best one.
For weakly informative priors — priors that rule out absurd values but do not strongly favor any particular answer — the posterior will be nearly identical to the OLS result. That is not a bug. It is a sanity check. The priors are doing their job: letting the data speak while gently constraining the model to reasonable territory.
Here is the generative model as a plate diagram:
\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\beta_0 &\sim \mathcal{N}(0, 200) \\
\beta_1 &\sim \mathcal{N}(0, 10) \\
\sigma &\sim \text{HalfNormal}(50) \\
\mu_i &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 \, x_i \\
y_i &\sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma) \quad \text{for } i = 1, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
}
\]
Each prior says: “I do not know the exact value, but I can rule out values that are physically nonsensical.” The normal priors on \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) are centered at zero with wide standard deviations. The half-normal prior on \(\sigma\) forces the noise scale to be positive. These are not strong opinions — they are guardrails.
A common worry at this stage is: “Am I not biasing the results by choosing priors?” With weakly informative priors like these, the answer is barely. The prior \(\mathcal{N}(0, 200)\) on the intercept says the intercept is somewhere between roughly \(-400\) and \(400\) — hardly a controversial claim for house prices in the hundreds of thousands. The data will overwhelm these priors. If you picked a prior of \(\mathcal{N}(0, 0.01)\), that would bias the results. The distinction between “weakly informative” and “informative” priors is one of the most important practical skills in Bayesian modeling, and we will explore it directly in Exercise 1.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.3 Building It in PyMC
Now we translate that generative model into PyMC code. Every line maps directly to a line in the math above.

import pymc as pm
import arviz as az

with pm.Model() as linear_model:
    # Priors
    beta_0 = pm.Normal("beta_0", mu=0, sigma=200)
    beta_1 = pm.Normal("beta_1", mu=0, sigma=10)
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal("sigma", sigma=50)

    # Expected value
    mu = beta_0 + beta_1 * df["sqft"].values

    # Likelihood
    y_obs = pm.Normal(
        "y_obs",
        mu=mu,
        sigma=sigma,
        observed=df["price"].values,
    )

    # Sample from the posterior
    trace = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
        progressbar=True,
    )




Let us walk through each piece:

pm.Normal("beta_0", mu=0, sigma=200) creates a random variable named beta_0 with a normal prior. The wide standard deviation of 200 means we are not strongly opinionated about the intercept.
pm.HalfNormal("sigma", sigma=50) creates a positive-valued random variable for the noise scale. HalfNormal is a normal distribution folded at zero — it only takes positive values, which is exactly what a standard deviation must be.
mu = beta_0 + beta_1 * df["sqft"].values is a deterministic transformation. PyMC tracks this computation through its graph, so the sampler knows how \(\mu\) depends on \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\).
pm.Normal("y_obs", ..., observed=df["price"].values) is the likelihood. The observed keyword pins this distribution to the actual data. This is what connects the model to reality.
pm.sample() runs the MCMC sampler (NUTS, by default) to draw samples from the posterior distribution. We draw 2000 samples per chain across 4 chains, giving us 8000 posterior draws in total.

Now check the diagnostics:

az.plot_trace(trace, var_names=["beta_0", "beta_1", "sigma"])
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 3.2: Trace plots for the Bayesian linear model. Left: posterior distributions. Right: MCMC chains (should look like fuzzy caterpillars).





The trace plots on the right should look like “fuzzy caterpillars” — all four chains mixing well, with no trends or stuck regions. The density plots on the left show the marginal posterior for each parameter.

az.summary(trace, var_names=["beta_0", "beta_1", "sigma"])

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.4 Comparing Frequentist and Bayesian Results
Now for the moment of truth. Let us put the OLS estimates and the Bayesian posterior means side by side.

posterior = trace.posterior

bayes_beta0 = float(posterior["beta_0"].mean())
bayes_beta1 = float(posterior["beta_1"].mean())
bayes_sigma = float(posterior["sigma"].mean())

ols_beta0 = ols_model.params[0]
ols_beta1 = ols_model.params[1]
ols_sigma = np.sqrt(ols_model.mse_resid)

comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "Parameter": ["Intercept (beta_0)", "Slope (beta_1)", "Sigma"],
    "True Value": [true_intercept, true_slope, noise_sd],
    "OLS Estimate": [
        f"{ols_beta0:.3f}",
        f"{ols_beta1:.4f}",
        f"{ols_sigma:.3f}",
    ],
    "Bayesian Posterior Mean": [
        f"{bayes_beta0:.3f}",
        f"{bayes_beta1:.4f}",
        f"{bayes_sigma:.3f}",
    ],
})
comparison








Parameter
True Value
OLS Estimate
Bayesian Posterior Mean




0
Intercept (beta_0)
50.00
34.153
33.844


1
Slope (beta_1)
0.15
0.1563
0.1565


2
Sigma
30.00
28.079
28.465







They should be nearly identical. With weakly informative priors and enough data, Bayesian estimation converges to the maximum likelihood estimate — which, for normal errors, is exactly OLS.






A quick sanity check for any Bayesian model



Chris Fonnesbeck offers a simple diagnostic: “The posterior should sit between the prior and the likelihood — if it does not, check your model.” For our linear model, the posterior means land between the prior means (zero, from our weakly informative priors) and the maximum likelihood estimates (the OLS values). Because the priors are wide and the data are plentiful, the posterior is pulled almost entirely toward the data. If the posterior were outside the range spanned by the prior and the likelihood — for instance, farther from zero than the OLS estimate despite a prior centred at zero — that would signal a coding error or a misspecified model. Get in the habit of checking this before interpreting results.


But the Bayesian version gives you something OLS does not (at least not as naturally): the full posterior distribution, not just point estimates. Let us compare the confidence intervals from OLS with the Highest Density Intervals (HDIs) from the posterior:

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 4))

params = ["beta_0", "beta_1", "sigma"]
ols_params = [ols_beta0, ols_beta1, ols_sigma]
ols_se = [ols_model.bse[0], ols_model.bse[1], None]
titles = ["Intercept", "Slope", "Sigma"]

for i, (pname, title) in enumerate(zip(params, titles)):
    ax = axes[i]
    # Bayesian HDI
    samples = posterior[pname].values.flatten()
    hdi = az.hdi(trace, var_names=[pname], hdi_prob=0.94)
    hdi_vals = hdi[pname].values

    ax.hist(
        samples,
        bins=50,
        density=True,
        alpha=0.4,
        color="orange",
        label="Posterior",
    )
    ax.axvline(
        float(np.mean(samples)),
        color="orange",
        linestyle="--",
        linewidth=1.5,
        label="Posterior mean",
    )
    ax.axvspan(
        hdi_vals[0],
        hdi_vals[1],
        alpha=0.15,
        color="orange",
        label="94% HDI",
    )

    # OLS point estimate and CI (for beta_0 and beta_1)
    if ols_se[i] is not None:
        ax.axvline(
            ols_params[i],
            color="steelblue",
            linestyle="-",
            linewidth=2,
            label="OLS estimate",
        )
        ci_low = ols_params[i] - 1.96 * ols_se[i]
        ci_high = ols_params[i] + 1.96 * ols_se[i]
        ax.axvspan(
            ci_low,
            ci_high,
            alpha=0.15,
            color="steelblue",
            label="OLS 95% CI",
        )

    ax.set_title(title)
    if i == 0:
        ax.legend(fontsize=7, loc="upper left")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 3.3: OLS confidence intervals (blue) vs. Bayesian 94% HDIs (orange) for each parameter.





For the intercept and slope, the blue (OLS) and orange (Bayesian) intervals overlap almost perfectly. That is reassuring. The two approaches agree when the problem is well-behaved. The Bayesian version also gives you a posterior for \(\sigma\), which OLS treats as a nuisance parameter you have to extract separately.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
section: 3.5 Why Bother Being Bayesian for Linear Regression?
text:
3.5 Why Bother Being Bayesian for Linear Regression?
If the answers are the same, why go through the trouble of priors and MCMC? Because the answers are the same only for simple, clean problems. The Bayesian framework pays off when things get messy:
1. Regularization via priors. Setting a tighter prior on \(\beta\) is mathematically equivalent to Ridge regression. A \(\mathcal{N}(0,
\tau^2)\) prior on the coefficients adds an \(L_2\) penalty of strength \(\sigma^2 / \tau^2\). But unlike Ridge, you do not need to tune a regularization hyperparameter — the data determine how much the prior should be trusted.
2. Full uncertainty propagation. When you predict a new observation, the Bayesian model propagates uncertainty through the entire chain: uncertainty in \(\beta_0\), in \(\beta_1\), in \(\sigma\), and the observation noise. OLS prediction intervals approximate this, but the Bayesian version handles it exactly even for small samples and nonlinear transformations.
3. Natural handling of small samples. With 10 data points, OLS confidence intervals rely on asymptotic approximations that may not hold. The Bayesian posterior is exact (given the model). Informative priors can stabilize estimates when data are scarce.
4. Easy extension to hierarchical models. Once you have a Bayesian linear model, adding group structure (different slopes for different cities, different intercepts for different products) is a matter of adding one more level of priors. We will see this in ?sec-hierarchical.
5. Robust alternatives. Replacing the Normal likelihood with a Student-\(t\) makes the model robust to outliers. In OLS, you would need to detect and remove outliers manually. In the Bayesian version, you swap one line of code.
The point is not that Bayesian linear regression is better than OLS for textbook problems. It is that the Bayesian framework is a superset: everything OLS can do, Bayesian regression can do too — and it extends gracefully to problems where OLS breaks down.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.6 Multiple Regression
Real models have more than one predictor. Let us add a second feature — number of bedrooms — and see how the PyMC model scales.

# Add bedrooms as a second predictor
bedrooms = rng.poisson(lam=2, size=n) + 1  # 1-6 bedrooms
true_beta_bed = 20.0
price_multi = (
    true_intercept
    + true_slope * sqft
    + true_beta_bed * bedrooms
    + rng.normal(0, noise_sd, size=n)
)

df_multi = pd.DataFrame({
    "sqft": sqft,
    "bedrooms": bedrooms,
    "price": price_multi,
})
df_multi.head()

3.6.1 Why centering and scaling helps MCMC
Before fitting, center and scale the predictors. This is not just good practice — it materially helps the MCMC sampler. When predictors are on different scales (square footage in the thousands, bedrooms in single digits), the posterior geometry becomes an elongated, tilted ellipse. The sampler has to take tiny steps to navigate it, leading to slow mixing and high autocorrelation.
Standardizing the predictors makes the posterior more spherical, which lets the sampler take bigger steps and explore efficiently.

# Standardize predictors
sqft_mean, sqft_sd = df_multi["sqft"].mean(), df_multi["sqft"].std()
bed_mean, bed_sd = df_multi["bedrooms"].mean(), df_multi["bedrooms"].std()

sqft_z = (df_multi["sqft"] - sqft_mean) / sqft_sd
bed_z = (df_multi["bedrooms"] - bed_mean) / bed_sd


with pm.Model() as multi_model:
    # Priors on standardized scale
    beta_0 = pm.Normal("beta_0", mu=0, sigma=200)
    beta_sqft = pm.Normal("beta_sqft", mu=0, sigma=50)
    beta_bed = pm.Normal("beta_bed", mu=0, sigma=50)
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal("sigma", sigma=50)

    mu = beta_0 + beta_sqft * sqft_z.values + beta_bed * bed_z.values

    y_obs = pm.Normal(
        "y_obs",
        mu=mu,
        sigma=sigma,
        observed=df_multi["price"].values,
    )

    trace_multi = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
    )





az.summary(
    trace_multi,
    var_names=["beta_0", "beta_sqft", "beta_bed", "sigma"],
)

Because the predictors are standardized, the coefficient magnitudes are directly comparable: a one-standard-deviation increase in square footage vs. a one-standard-deviation increase in bedrooms. To recover the coefficients on the original scale, back-transform: \(\beta_{\text{orig}} = \beta_{\text{std}} / \text{sd}(x)\).

post = trace_multi.posterior
slope_sqft_orig = post["beta_sqft"] / sqft_sd
slope_bed_orig = post["beta_bed"] / bed_sd

print(f"Slope (sqft), original scale:    "
      f"{float(slope_sqft_orig.mean()):.4f}")

      f"{float(slope_bed_orig.mean()):.2f}")

Slope (sqft), original scale:    0.1504
Slope (bedrooms), original scale: 24.12
True slope (sqft):                0.15
True slope (bedrooms):            20.0


The pattern for adding more predictors is the same: add a prior, add a term to \(\mu\). The model scales linearly with the number of features.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.7 Bayesian Logistic Regression
Not all outcomes are continuous. When the response is binary — yes/no, click/no-click, survived/died — we need logistic regression. If you have used scikit-learn’s LogisticRegression, you have already fit this model. The idea is the same as linear regression: a linear combination of predictors determines the outcome. But the outcome is a probability bounded between 0 and 1, so we pass the linear combination through the logistic (sigmoid) function:
\[
p_i = \text{logit}^{-1}(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i)}}
\]
\[
y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i)
\]
Let us simulate a dataset where the probability of passing an exam depends on hours of study:

# Simulate logistic data: P(pass) depends on hours studied
n_students = 120
hours = rng.uniform(0, 10, size=n_students)
true_b0_log = -3.0
true_b1_log = 0.8
logit_p = true_b0_log + true_b1_log * hours
prob_pass = 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-logit_p))
passed = rng.binomial(n=1, p=prob_pass)

print(f"Pass rate: {passed.mean():.2%}")

Pass rate: 63.33%



with pm.Model() as logistic_model:
    b0 = pm.Normal("b0", mu=0, sigma=5)
    b1 = pm.Normal("b1", mu=0, sigma=5)

    # Linear predictor passed through sigmoid
    p = pm.math.sigmoid(b0 + b1 * hours)

    # Bernoulli likelihood
    y_obs = pm.Bernoulli("y_obs", p=p, observed=passed)

    trace_logistic = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
    )





az.summary(trace_logistic, var_names=["b0", "b1"])

Now compare the Bayesian result to scikit-learn’s LogisticRegression:

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# Sklearn fit
lr = LogisticRegression(penalty=None, solver="lbfgs")
lr.fit(hours.reshape(-1, 1), passed)
sk_b0 = lr.intercept_[0]
sk_b1 = lr.coef_[0, 0]

# Plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(
    hours,
    passed,
    alpha=0.3,
    s=30,
    color="steelblue",
    label="Data",
)

x_grid = np.linspace(0, 10, 200)

# Posterior samples of the probability curve
post_log = trace_logistic.posterior
b0_samples = post_log["b0"].values.flatten()
b1_samples = post_log["b1"].values.flatten()
idx = rng.choice(len(b0_samples), size=200, replace=False)
for j in idx:
    p_curve = 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-(b0_samples[j] + b1_samples[j] * x_grid)))
    ax.plot(x_grid, p_curve, color="gray", alpha=0.03, linewidth=0.8)

# Posterior mean curve
p_mean = 1.0 / (
    1.0 + np.exp(-(b0_samples.mean() + b1_samples.mean() * x_grid))
)
ax.plot(x_grid, p_mean, color="black", linewidth=2, label="Posterior mean")

# Sklearn curve
p_sk = 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-(sk_b0 + sk_b1 * x_grid)))
ax.plot(
    x_grid,
    p_sk,
    color="red",
    linewidth=2,
    linestyle="--",
    label="sklearn MLE",
)

ax.set_xlabel("Hours studied")
ax.set_ylabel("P(pass)")
ax.set_title("Bayesian Logistic Regression: Posterior Probability Curves")
ax.legend(loc="lower right")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 3.4: Bayesian posterior over the probability curve (gray lines = posterior samples, black = posterior mean). Red dashed = sklearn point estimate.





The gray spaghetti of posterior samples shows the uncertainty in the probability curve. Where data are sparse (very low or very high hours), the curves fan out — the model is less sure. Where data are plentiful, they converge. The sklearn point estimate (red dashed) sits right in the middle. Again: same answer, but the Bayesian version tells you how much to trust it.
This spaghetti plot is one of the most useful visualizations in Bayesian analysis. Every gray line represents one plausible logistic curve drawn from the posterior. A tight bundle of lines means the model is confident; a wide fan means it is not. For a decision-maker — say, a university administrator deciding how many study hours to recommend — the spread of those curves is just as important as their center. The frequentist approach gives you a single curve and a confidence interval table. The Bayesian approach gives you a picture you can look at and immediately understand.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.8 Robust Regression
Real-world data have outliers. OLS treats every residual equally, so a single extreme point can drag the entire regression line. The classic frequentist fix is to detect outliers (Cook’s distance, studentized residuals) and remove them. The Bayesian fix is more elegant: replace the Normal likelihood with a Student-\(t\) distribution.
The Student-\(t\) has heavier tails than the Normal. That means it assigns more probability to extreme values, so outliers are “expected” by the model rather than treated as anomalies. The key parameter is the degrees of freedom \(\nu\): small \(\nu\) means heavy tails (robust); as \(\nu \to \infty\), the \(t\) converges to the Normal.
Let us see this in action with contaminated data:

# Generate clean data plus 5% contamination
n_robust = 100
x_robust = rng.uniform(0, 10, size=n_robust)
y_clean = 10 + 3 * x_robust + rng.normal(0, 3, size=n_robust)

# Add 5% outliers
n_outliers = int(0.05 * n_robust)
outlier_idx = rng.choice(n_robust, size=n_outliers, replace=False)
y_contaminated = y_clean.copy()
y_contaminated[outlier_idx] += rng.uniform(30, 60, size=n_outliers)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(
    x_robust,
    y_contaminated,
    alpha=0.6,
    s=30,
    color="steelblue",
)
ax.scatter(
    x_robust[outlier_idx],
    y_contaminated[outlier_idx],
    color="red",
    s=60,
    zorder=5,
    label=f"Outliers ({n_outliers} points)",
    edgecolor="black",
    linewidth=0.5,
)
ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("Data with 5% contamination")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Now fit two models — one with a Normal likelihood, one with a Student-\(t\) likelihood — and compare:

# Model 1: Normal likelihood (sensitive to outliers)
with pm.Model() as normal_model:
    b0 = pm.Normal("b0", mu=0, sigma=50)
    b1 = pm.Normal("b1", mu=0, sigma=20)
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal("sigma", sigma=20)
    mu = b0 + b1 * x_robust
    y_obs = pm.Normal("y_obs", mu=mu, sigma=sigma, observed=y_contaminated)
    trace_normal = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
    )

# Model 2: Student-t likelihood (robust to outliers)
with pm.Model() as robust_model:
    b0 = pm.Normal("b0", mu=0, sigma=50)
    b1 = pm.Normal("b1", mu=0, sigma=20)
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal("sigma", sigma=20)
    nu = pm.Exponential("nu", lam=1 / 30)  # prior on degrees of freedom
    mu = b0 + b1 * x_robust
    y_obs = pm.StudentT(
        "y_obs",
        nu=nu,
        mu=mu,
        sigma=sigma,
        observed=y_contaminated,
    )
    trace_robust = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
    )








fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(
    x_robust,
    y_contaminated,
    alpha=0.5,
    s=30,
    color="steelblue",
)
ax.scatter(
    x_robust[outlier_idx],
    y_contaminated[outlier_idx],
    color="red",
    s=60,
    zorder=5,
    edgecolor="black",
    linewidth=0.5,
)

x_line = np.linspace(0, 10, 200)

# Normal model line
post_n = trace_normal.posterior
b0_n = float(post_n["b0"].mean())
b1_n = float(post_n["b1"].mean())
ax.plot(
    x_line,
    b0_n + b1_n * x_line,
    color="steelblue",
    linewidth=2.5,
    label=f"Normal: y = {b0_n:.1f} + {b1_n:.2f}x",
)

# Robust model line
post_r = trace_robust.posterior
b0_r = float(post_r["b0"].mean())
b1_r = float(post_r["b1"].mean())
ax.plot(
    x_line,
    b0_r + b1_r * x_line,
    color="orange",
    linewidth=2.5,
    label=f"Student-t: y = {b0_r:.1f} + {b1_r:.2f}x",
)

# True line
ax.plot(
    x_line,
    10 + 3 * x_line,
    color="black",
    linewidth=1.5,
    linestyle="--",
    label="True: y = 10 + 3x",
)

ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("Normal vs. Robust Regression")
ax.legend(loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 3.5: Normal vs. Student-t regression on contaminated data. The robust model (orange) is less influenced by the outliers.





The Normal model (blue) gets pulled toward the outliers — both the slope and intercept are biased. The Student-\(t\) model (orange) shrugs off the contamination and recovers a line much closer to the truth (black dashed). The only change was swapping pm.Normal for pm.StudentT and adding a prior on \(\nu\). One line of code buys you robustness that would take pages of diagnostics in the frequentist approach.
This is worth pausing on, because it illustrates a broader principle. In classical statistics, robustness is achieved by detecting and removing problematic observations — a two-step process that requires judgment calls about which points to exclude. In Bayesian modeling, robustness is achieved by choosing a better model — one that accounts for the possibility of extreme values from the start. The outliers are not removed; they are simply given less influence because the model expects them. This is a fundamentally different philosophy: instead of cleaning data to fit the model, you adjust the model to fit the data.






Why heavy tails matter: a lesson from finance



Thomas Wiecki puts it bluntly: “The Normal distribution assumes 2008-scale crashes never happen; the Student-\(t\) is more honest.” Under a Gaussian likelihood, a six-sigma event has a probability on the order of \(10^{-9}\). In financial data, such events happen every few years. The Normal model is not just slightly wrong — it is systematically blind to the most consequential observations.
The practical advice is simple: whenever your data could plausibly contain extreme values — financial returns, insurance claims, network traffic, sensor readings in harsh environments — default to a heavy-tailed likelihood. The Student-\(t\) with a learned \(\nu\) is the easiest upgrade: if the data are well behaved, \(\nu\) will be estimated large and the model reduces to a Normal. If the data have genuine heavy tails, \(\nu\) shrinks and the model accommodates them. You pay almost nothing for this insurance.


Let us check the inferred degrees of freedom:

nu_samples = trace_robust.posterior["nu"].values.flatten()
print(f"Posterior mean of nu: {nu_samples.mean():.1f}")
print(f"94% HDI of nu: [{np.percentile(nu_samples, 3):.1f}, "
      f"{np.percentile(nu_samples, 97):.1f}]")

Posterior mean of nu: 1.4
94% HDI of nu: [1.0, 2.1]


A small \(\nu\) (say, below 10) tells you the model found evidence of heavy tails — meaning the outliers are real, not just quirks of the Normal assumption. A large \(\nu\) (above 30) means the data are well-behaved and the \(t\) is essentially acting like a Normal.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.9 Prediction with Uncertainty
One of the most practical things you can do with a Bayesian model is make predictions that carry uncertainty. Not “the predicted price is $350k” but “the predicted price is $350k, with a 95% chance of being between $290k and $410k.”
PyMC provides pm.sample_posterior_predictive() for exactly this. It takes each posterior draw of the parameters, plugs them into the likelihood, and generates a simulated \(y\) value. The result is the posterior predictive distribution: the full distribution of outcomes you might see for new data, given everything the model has learned.

# Predict on a grid of new sqft values using the simple model
sqft_new = np.linspace(600, 3200, 100)

# Use the already-fitted trace to generate predictions
# by computing mu and adding noise from the posterior
beta_0_s = trace.posterior["beta_0"].values.flatten()
beta_1_s = trace.posterior["beta_1"].values.flatten()
sigma_s = trace.posterior["sigma"].values.flatten()

# For each posterior sample, compute predicted y at new sqft
y_pred_all = np.zeros((len(beta_0_s), len(sqft_new)))
for i in range(len(beta_0_s)):
    mu_i = beta_0_s[i] + beta_1_s[i] * sqft_new
    y_pred_all[i] = rng.normal(mu_i, sigma_s[i])


y_pred = y_pred_all

pred_mean = y_pred.mean(axis=0)
pred_lower = np.percentile(y_pred, 2.5, axis=0)
pred_upper = np.percentile(y_pred, 97.5, axis=0)
pred_10 = np.percentile(y_pred, 10, axis=0)
pred_90 = np.percentile(y_pred, 90, axis=0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(
    df["sqft"],
    df["price"],
    alpha=0.5,
    s=30,
    color="steelblue",
    label="Observed data",
)
ax.plot(
    sqft_new,
    pred_mean,
    color="black",
    linewidth=2,
    label="Posterior mean",
)
ax.fill_between(
    sqft_new,
    pred_lower,
    pred_upper,
    alpha=0.15,
    color="orange",
    label="95% prediction interval",
)
ax.fill_between(
    sqft_new,
    pred_10,
    pred_90,
    alpha=0.25,
    color="orange",
    label="80% prediction interval",
)
ax.set_xlabel("Square footage")
ax.set_ylabel("Price ($k)")
ax.set_title("Bayesian Predictions with Uncertainty")
ax.legend(loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 3.6: Posterior predictive distribution: the shaded band shows the 95% prediction interval, accounting for both parameter uncertainty and observation noise.





Notice two sources of uncertainty in the prediction band:

Parameter uncertainty — we are not sure exactly where the regression line sits (this is the uncertainty in \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\)).
Observation noise — even if we knew the line perfectly, new observations scatter around it according to \(\sigma\).

The posterior predictive distribution captures both. OLS prediction intervals do something similar, but they rely on normality assumptions and large-sample approximations. The Bayesian version works exactly the same way whether you have 10 observations or 10,000.
In practice, posterior predictive checks are also a powerful model diagnostic. If your prediction intervals are too narrow (real data falls outside them too often), the model is overconfident — maybe you need heavier tails or more predictors. If the intervals are too wide, the model is not learning enough from the data — maybe your priors are too strong or you are missing a key feature. We will return to this idea in ?sec-bayesian-workflow.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.10 Exercises
Work through these exercises to solidify your understanding. Solutions are provided below each exercise block.

3.10.1 Exercise 1: Prior sensitivity
Refit the simple linear model from ?sec-building-it-in-pymc with a much tighter prior on the slope: pm.Normal("beta_1", mu=0, sigma=0.01). Compare the posterior mean for \(\beta_1\) to the OLS estimate. What happened, and why?






Solution






with pm.Model() as tight_prior_model:
    beta_0 = pm.Normal("beta_0", mu=0, sigma=200)
    beta_1 = pm.Normal("beta_1", mu=0, sigma=0.01)  # very tight!
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal("sigma", sigma=50)
    mu = beta_0 + beta_1 * df["sqft"].values
    y_obs = pm.Normal(
        "y_obs",
        mu=mu,
        sigma=sigma,
        observed=df["price"].values,
    )
    trace_tight = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
    )

post_tight = trace_tight.posterior
print(f"Tight prior -- beta_1 posterior mean: "
      f"{float(post_tight['beta_1'].mean()):.6f}")
print(f"OLS estimate:                         "
      f"{ols_beta1:.6f}")
print(f"True value:                           "
      f"{true_slope:.6f}")




Tight prior -- beta_1 posterior mean: 0.073652
OLS estimate:                         0.156347
True value:                           0.150000


The tight prior pulls \(\beta_1\) toward zero. With \(\sigma = 0.01\), the prior says “I am 95% sure the slope is between \(-0.02\) and \(0.02\).” The data disagree (the true slope is 0.15), but the prior is so strong that the posterior compromises. This is prior-data conflict — the prior overwhelms the evidence. The lesson: wide priors let the data speak; narrow priors encode strong beliefs that had better be right.





3.10.2 Exercise 2: Poisson regression
Fit a Bayesian Poisson regression to model the number of bedrooms as a function of square footage. Use pm.Poisson for the likelihood and a log link: \(\log(\lambda_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i\). Does larger square footage predict more bedrooms?






Solution






import pytensor.tensor as pt

with pm.Model() as poisson_model:
    b0 = pm.Normal("b0", mu=0, sigma=5)
    b1 = pm.Normal("b1", mu=0, sigma=5)

    # Standardize sqft for better sampling
    sqft_std = (df_multi["sqft"].values - sqft_mean) / sqft_sd
    log_lambda = b0 + b1 * sqft_std
    lam = pt.exp(log_lambda)

    y_obs = pm.Poisson(
        "y_obs",
        mu=lam,
        observed=df_multi["bedrooms"].values,
    )

    trace_poisson = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
    )

print(az.summary(trace_poisson, var_names=["b0", "b1"]))




     mean     sd  hdi_3%  hdi_97%  mcse_mean  mcse_sd  ess_bulk  ess_tail  \
b0  1.094  0.065   0.971    1.215      0.001    0.001    7373.0    5473.0   
b1  0.026  0.067  -0.099    0.153      0.001    0.001    7312.0    6006.0   

    r_hat  
b0    1.0  
b1    1.0  


If the HDI for \(\beta_1\) excludes zero, larger square footage is associated with more bedrooms (as we would expect). The Poisson likelihood naturally handles count data — no need to pretend bedrooms are continuous.





3.10.3 Exercise 3: Model comparison
Fit both the Normal and Student-\(t\) models to the clean data (without outliers) from the robust regression section. Use az.compare() with WAIC or LOO to compare them. Which model is preferred when there are no outliers, and why?






Solution






# Fit both models to clean data
with pm.Model() as clean_normal:
    b0 = pm.Normal("b0", mu=0, sigma=50)
    b1 = pm.Normal("b1", mu=0, sigma=20)
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal("sigma", sigma=20)
    mu = b0 + b1 * x_robust
    y_obs = pm.Normal("y_obs", mu=mu, sigma=sigma, observed=y_clean)
    trace_cn = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
        idata_kwargs={"log_likelihood": True},
    )

with pm.Model() as clean_robust:
    b0 = pm.Normal("b0", mu=0, sigma=50)
    b1 = pm.Normal("b1", mu=0, sigma=20)
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal("sigma", sigma=20)
    nu = pm.Exponential("nu", lam=1 / 30)
    mu = b0 + b1 * x_robust
    y_obs = pm.StudentT(
        "y_obs",
        nu=nu,
        mu=mu,
        sigma=sigma,
        observed=y_clean,
    )
    trace_cr = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=1000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=42,
        idata_kwargs={"log_likelihood": True},
    )

comp = az.compare(
    {"Normal": trace_cn, "Student-t": trace_cr},
    ic="loo",
)
print(comp)







           rank    elpd_loo     p_loo  elpd_diff    weight        se  \
Student-t     0 -245.474337  3.031162   0.000000  0.545971  7.647937   
Normal        1 -245.489526  2.867452   0.015189  0.454029  7.591713   

                dse  warning scale  
Student-t  0.000000    False   log  
Normal     0.554093    False   log  


When the data are clean, the Normal model should be preferred (or the two should be nearly tied). The Student-\(t\) model has an extra parameter (\(\nu\)) that does not help when there are no outliers, so it gets penalized for complexity. This is the principle of parsimony at work: do not add flexibility you do not need.





3.10.4 Exercise 4: Predict a new house
Using the simple Bayesian linear model, predict the price of a 2000 square-foot house. Report the posterior predictive mean and the 94% HDI of the prediction.






Solution






# Predict for a single house using posterior samples
sqft_single = 2000.0
mu_new = beta_0_s + beta_1_s * sqft_single
y_new = rng.normal(mu_new, sigma_s)
print(f"Predicted price for 2000 sqft house:")
print(f"  Posterior predictive mean: ${y_new.mean():.1f}k")
hdi_94 = az.hdi(y_new, hdi_prob=0.94)
print(f"  94% HDI: [${hdi_94[0]:.1f}k, ${hdi_94[1]:.1f}k]")
print(f"  True expected price: "
      f"${true_intercept + true_slope * 2000:.1f}k")

Predicted price for 2000 sqft house:
  Posterior predictive mean: $346.6k
  94% HDI: [$293.9k, $403.3k]
  True expected price: $350.0k


The posterior predictive mean should be close to the true expected value of \(50 + 0.15 \times 2000 = 350\). The 94% HDI captures the range of plausible prices, reflecting both parameter uncertainty and observation noise. This is the Bayesian answer to “what price should I expect?” — a full distribution, not a single number.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian
text:
3.11 Summary
This chapter covered a lot of ground, but the core message is simple. The Bayesian linear model is the same linear model you already know, dressed up with priors and solved with MCMC instead of algebra. For clean data with enough observations, the Bayesian and frequentist answers agree. The Bayesian version earns its keep when you need robustness (Student-\(t\)), uncertainty propagation (posterior predictive), regularization (informative priors), or extensibility (hierarchical models in the next chapter).
The pattern you learned here — define priors, write the likelihood, sample, check diagnostics, interpret — is the same pattern for every PyMC model in this book. The models get more complex; the workflow does not.
crumbs: 3  Linear Models: From Frequentist to Bayesian

title: 4  Hierarchical and Multilevel Models
text:
4.1 The Problem: Too Little or Too Much Pooling
You have data from twenty groups. Some groups have hundreds of observations; others have five. You need an estimate for every group. What do you do?
Option A: ignore the groups entirely and estimate one number for everyone. Option B: estimate each group separately, pretending the others don’t exist. Both options are wrong—and the way they are wrong tells you exactly why hierarchical models exist.
This chapter builds a hierarchical model from scratch. You will see why the “just estimate each group” approach produces wild estimates for small groups, why “lump everyone together” throws away real differences, and how partial pooling threads the needle between the two. Along the way, you will learn the centered vs. non-centered parameterization trick that solves the most common convergence problem in hierarchical models, how to visualize shrinkage so you can explain partial pooling to your team, and how to extend the basic model with varying slopes when the effect of a predictor genuinely differs across groups.
By the end, you will be able to build, diagnose, and interpret hierarchical models in PyMC for any grouped data you encounter.
Imagine you run a network of twenty websites. Each site gets a different volume of traffic and a different conversion rate. Your boss asks: “What is the true conversion rate for each site?”
Let’s simulate this scenario. We will generate data from twenty sites with true conversion rates drawn from a common distribution, but with wildly different sample sizes—some sites have thousands of visitors, others have only a handful.
n_sites = 20
true_mu = 0.08        # population mean conversion rate: 8%
true_kappa = 50       # concentration: controls spread across sites

# True per-site rates from a Beta distribution
alpha_true = true_mu * true_kappa
beta_true = (1 - true_mu) * true_kappa
true_rates = rng.beta(a=alpha_true, b=beta_true, size=n_sites)

# Sample sizes: some sites are big, some are tiny
sample_sizes = rng.choice(
    [10, 15, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000],
    size=n_sites,
    replace=True,
)

# Observed conversions
conversions = rng.binomial(n=sample_sizes, p=true_rates)

site_names = [f"Site {i+1}" for i in range(n_sites)]
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "site": site_names,
    "visitors": sample_sizes,
    "conversions": conversions,
    "observed_rate": conversions / sample_sizes,
    "true_rate": true_rates,
})
df.sort_values("visitors", inplace=True, ignore_index=True)

title: 4  Hierarchical and Multilevel Models
text:
4.1.1 Complete pooling: one estimate for all
Complete pooling ignores the group labels entirely. It computes a single conversion rate from the combined data:

pooled_rate = df["conversions"].sum() / df["visitors"].sum()
print(f"Pooled estimate: {pooled_rate:.4f}")

Pooled estimate: 0.0792


This number is the same for every site. It is stable—no wild estimates for small groups—but it throws away all the real differences between sites. A site with a true 15% conversion rate gets the same estimate as one with 3%. That is not useful.
In statistics, this is called complete pooling: you pool all the data into a single bucket and estimate one parameter. It maximizes the amount of data behind the estimate, but it treats genuinely different groups as identical.


4.1.2 No pooling: separate estimates per group
No pooling treats each site independently. The estimate for each group is just its own conversions / visitors:

print("No-pooling estimates (sorted by sample size):\n")
for _, row in df.iterrows():
    print(
        f"  {row['site']:>8s}: "
        f"{row['observed_rate']:.3f}  "
        f"(n={row['visitors']:>4d}, "
        f"true={row['true_rate']:.3f})"
    )

No-pooling estimates (sorted by sample size):

    Site 2: 0.100  (n=  10, true=0.079)
    Site 3: 0.100  (n=  10, true=0.109)
    Site 4: 0.000  (n=  10, true=0.088)
   Site 10: 0.067  (n=  15, true=0.121)
   Site 14: 0.067  (n=  15, true=0.061)
   Site 19: 0.067  (n=  15, true=0.084)
    Site 1: 0.060  (n=  50, true=0.078)
   Site 20: 0.080  (n=  50, true=0.093)
    Site 8: 0.020  (n= 100, true=0.053)
   Site 12: 0.070  (n= 100, true=0.100)
   Site 15: 0.060  (n= 100, true=0.060)
   Site 17: 0.050  (n= 100, true=0.055)
   Site 18: 0.010  (n= 100, true=0.042)
    Site 9: 0.102  (n= 500, true=0.079)
    Site 6: 0.122  (n= 500, true=0.132)
    Site 5: 0.096  (n= 500, true=0.112)
   Site 16: 0.076  (n= 500, true=0.073)
    Site 7: 0.072  (n= 500, true=0.090)
   Site 11: 0.051  (n=2000, true=0.052)
   Site 13: 0.099  (n=2000, true=0.104)


Scan the output. Sites with large sample sizes (500+) have estimates close to the truth. But small sites are all over the place—a site with 10 visitors and 0 conversions gets an estimate of 0.0%, and one with 10 visitors and 2 conversions gets 20%. These estimates are technically unbiased, but they are noisy enough to be useless for decision-making.
This is no pooling: each group is estimated in complete isolation. It respects group differences perfectly, but it uses minimal data for each estimate. When a group has five observations, you are estimating a rate from five coin flips—the variance is enormous.
The core tension is now clear. Complete pooling has low variance but high bias (it ignores real differences). No pooling has zero bias but high variance (small groups get unreliable estimates). We need a method that navigates between the two extremes, borrowing just enough information from the other groups to stabilize small estimates without flattening real differences.
Let’s visualize both approaches:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
x = np.arange(n_sites)

ax.scatter(
    x, df["true_rate"], marker="D", s=60,
    color="C2", zorder=3, label="True rate",
)
ax.scatter(
    x, df["observed_rate"],
    s=df["visitors"] / 5, alpha=0.6,
    color="C0", zorder=2, label="No-pooling estimate",
)
ax.axhline(
    pooled_rate, color="C3", ls="--", lw=1.5,
    label=f"Pooled estimate ({pooled_rate:.3f})",
)
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(
    [f"{s}\n(n={n})" for s, n in
     zip(df["site"], df["visitors"])],
    fontsize=7, rotation=45, ha="right",
)
ax.set_ylabel("Conversion rate")
ax.set_title("Complete pooling vs. no pooling")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 4.1: Complete pooling (dashed line) ignores group differences. No pooling (dots) is noisy for small groups.





The dot sizes are proportional to sample size. Notice how the smallest dots deviate the most from the true diamonds. This is the core problem that hierarchical models solve.

title: 4  Hierarchical and Multilevel Models
text:
4.2 Partial Pooling: The Bayesian Solution
A hierarchical model says: “These twenty sites are different, but they are not completely different. They all sell similar products, they all share a brand, and their true conversion rates probably live in some common range.”
Instead of estimating each site in isolation, the model learns the common range (the population distribution) and the individual site rates simultaneously. The result is partial pooling:

Sites with lots of data keep their own estimates, barely affected by the other sites.
Sites with little data get pulled (“shrunk”) toward the population mean, because the population mean is more informative than their noisy handful of observations.

This is not a hack or a bias—it is the mathematically optimal trade-off between using all the information you have (the group structure) and respecting what each group’s data actually says.
Think of it like this. You move to a new city and try a restaurant. The food is terrible. How much do you update your belief about all restaurants in this city? Not much, because you know that cities generally have a range of restaurants. Your single bad meal gets “shrunk” toward your prior expectation of restaurant quality. But if you eat at ten restaurants and they are all terrible, your estimate for the city stands on its own—you have enough data to overwhelm the prior.
That is exactly what a hierarchical model does, automatically, for every group at once.
The term “hierarchical” comes from the structure: parameters are organized in levels. Observations live at the bottom level. Group parameters sit in the middle. And the population distribution—the “hyperparameters”—sits on top. Information flows both up (data informs the population) and down (the population regularizes the groups). Other names for the same idea include multilevel models, mixed-effects models, and random-effects models. The concepts are identical; the names reflect different disciplinary traditions.

title: 4  Hierarchical and Multilevel Models
text:
4.3 The Math (Light Version)
A two-level hierarchical model has three layers:
Layer 1: The population. There is a population-level mean \(\mu\) and spread \(\tau\) that describe how group parameters vary across groups. These are called hyperparameters because they sit above the group-level parameters.
Layer 2: The groups. Each group \(j\) has its own parameter \(\theta_j\), drawn from the population distribution: \[
\theta_j \sim \text{Normal}(\mu, \tau), \quad j = 1, \ldots, J
\]
Layer 3: The observations. Within each group, observations are generated from the group’s parameter: \[
y_{ij} \sim \text{Normal}(\theta_j, \sigma), \quad i = 1, \ldots, n_j
\]
The key insight is that \(\mu\) and \(\tau\) are not fixed—they are learned from the data across all groups. When the model estimates \(\theta_j\) for a small group, it borrows information from the other groups through \(\mu\) and \(\tau\). This is what makes it “hierarchical”: the parameters live at different levels of a hierarchy.

4.3.1 The shrinkage factor
How much does each group get pulled toward the population mean? The amount of shrinkage for group \(j\) is approximately: \[
\hat{\theta}_j \approx
\underbrace{\frac{n_j / \sigma^2}{n_j / \sigma^2 + 1/\tau^2}}_{\text{weight on data}} \cdot \bar{y}_j
\;+\;
\underbrace{\frac{1/\tau^2}{n_j / \sigma^2 + 1/\tau^2}}_{\text{weight on population}} \cdot \mu
\]
Read this as a weighted average between the group’s own data (\(\bar{y}_j\)) and the population mean (\(\mu\)). The weights depend on two things:

Group sample size \(n_j\). More data → more weight on the group’s own average → less shrinkage.
Population spread \(\tau\). When \(\tau\) is small (groups are genuinely similar), the population mean gets more weight. When \(\tau\) is large (groups are very different), each group’s data dominates.

This weighted average is not something you compute by hand. The MCMC sampler discovers it automatically when it fits the model. But understanding the formula helps you predict what the model will do: small groups shrink a lot; big groups barely move.
Another way to think about hierarchical models: they implement an adaptive form of regularization. In machine learning, you might add an L2 penalty to shrink all coefficients toward zero. A hierarchical model shrinks group parameters toward the group mean instead of zero, and the amount of shrinkage is learned from the data, not set by a hyperparameter grid search. Thomas Wiecki, one of PyMC’s core developers, frames it this way: “One way to view hierarchical models is as a form of regularization—you shrink everything toward the group mean, not zero.” That distinction matters. L2 regularization assumes that the “default” parameter value is zero, which is arbitrary. Hierarchical shrinkage pulls toward the empirical group mean—a much more informative anchor, especially when some groups have little data.

title: 4  Hierarchical and Multilevel Models
section: 4.4 Building a Hierarchical Model in PyMC
text:
4.4 Building a Hierarchical Model in PyMC
The classic example for hierarchical models comes from Andrew Gelman’s radon dataset. Radon is a radioactive gas that seeps into homes from the ground; the EPA measured radon levels in homes across Minnesota, grouped by county. Some counties have many measurements; others have just one or two.
The question: what is the typical radon level in each county?

# Load the radon dataset from ArviZ
radon_data = az.load_arviz_data("radon")

# If the built-in dataset is not available, we construct
# a representative subset. The structure is what matters:
# homes nested within counties, with a floor measurement.
try:
    # Use the srrs2 data used in Gelman & Hill
    import ssl
    ssl._create_default_https_context = ssl._create_unverified_context
    srrs2 = pd.read_csv(
        "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pymc-devs/"
        "pymc-examples/main/examples/data/srrs2.dat"
    )
    srrs_mn = srrs2[srrs2.state == "MN"].copy()
    srrs_mn["log_radon"] = np.log1p(srrs_mn.activity)
    # Keep counties with at least 1 observation
    county_counts = srrs_mn.county.value_counts()
    keep = county_counts.index.tolist()
    srrs_mn = srrs_mn[srrs_mn.county.isin(keep)].copy()
    srrs_mn["county_idx"] = pd.Categorical(
        srrs_mn.county
    ).codes
    counties = srrs_mn.county.unique()
    n_counties = len(counties)
    print(
        f"Loaded {len(srrs_mn)} observations "
        f"from {n_counties} counties"
    )
except Exception:
    # Fallback: simulate radon-like data
    n_counties = 40
    counties = [f"county_{i}" for i in range(n_counties)]
    county_sizes = rng.integers(1, 50, size=n_counties)
    county_idx = np.repeat(
        np.arange(n_counties), county_sizes
    )
    true_county_means = rng.normal(
        loc=1.3, scale=0.4, size=n_counties
    )
    log_radon = rng.normal(
        loc=true_county_means[county_idx], scale=0.7
    )
    srrs_mn = pd.DataFrame({
        "county": np.array(counties)[county_idx],
        "county_idx": county_idx,
        "log_radon": log_radon,
        "floor": rng.integers(0, 2, size=len(log_radon)),
    })
    print(
        f"Simulated {len(srrs_mn)} observations "
        f"from {n_counties} counties"
    )

Loaded 919 observations from 85 counties


Now build the hierarchical model. Every line is annotated:

county_idx = srrs_mn["county_idx"].values
log_radon_obs = srrs_mn["log_radon"].values

coords = {"county": counties, "obs": np.arange(len(log_radon_obs))}

with pm.Model(coords=coords) as hierarchical_model:

    # --- Hyperpriors (population level) ---
    # mu_alpha: the average log-radon across ALL counties.
    # We give it a vague Normal prior centered on 1.4
    # (roughly the log of typical radon in pCi/L).
    mu_alpha = pm.Normal("mu_alpha", mu=1.4, sigma=1.0)

    # sigma_alpha: how much county means spread around
    # the population mean. HalfNormal forces it positive.
    sigma_alpha = pm.HalfNormal("sigma_alpha", sigma=1.0)

    # --- Group-level priors (county level) ---
    # Each county gets its own intercept, drawn from the
    # population distribution. dims="county" labels the
    # parameter so ArviZ plots are readable.
    alpha = pm.Normal(
        "alpha",
        mu=mu_alpha,
        sigma=sigma_alpha,
        dims="county",
    )

    # --- Observation-level noise ---
    sigma_y = pm.HalfNormal("sigma_y", sigma=1.0)

    # --- Likelihood ---
    # Each home's log-radon is Normal around its county mean.
    y_obs = pm.Normal(
        "y_obs",
        mu=alpha[county_idx],
        sigma=sigma_y,
        observed=log_radon_obs,
        dims="obs",
    )

pm.model_to_graphviz(hierarchical_model)








Let’s walk through the structure:

mu_alpha and sigma_alpha are the hyperparameters. They describe the population of county means. The model learns these from the data.
alpha is a vector of county-level intercepts, one per county. Each one is drawn from Normal(mu_alpha, sigma_alpha). This is where the partial pooling happens: counties with little data get pulled toward mu_alpha.
sigma_y is the within-county noise.
y_obs is the likelihood, linking each observation to its county’s intercept.

The coords and dims arguments are not required, but they make ArviZ output much easier to read. Instead of seeing alpha[17] in a trace plot, you see alpha[Hennepin].
Now sample:

with hierarchical_model:
    idata_hierarchical = pm.sample(
        draws=2000,
        tune=2000,
        chains=4,
        random_seed=rng,
        target_accept=0.9,
    )

UserWarning: install "ipywidgets" for Jupyter support

az.summary(
    idata_hierarchical,
    var_names=["mu_alpha", "sigma_alpha", "sigma_y"],
    round_to=3,
)
